AI-Linked Borrowing Likely to Grow From Healthy Levels — Market Talk
data.description: Companies are expected to issue more debt to finance AI projects, BlackRock Investment Institute's Wei Li says at the 2026 outlook media roundtable. "Leverage is starting to go higher, but we come from a place of very manageable kind of indebtedness," she says. Governments have greater level of indebtedness compared to the private sector, Li says. Companies are likely to rely on the private sector, rather than government funding, to finance AI investments, she says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: ViaCon enters into an agreement regarding the disposal of the property in St Helens, UK
data.description:
In June 2025, ViaCon announced the signing of a letter of intent regarding the sale of its property in St Helens. ViaCon has now entered into a definitive agreement for the transaction, which is expected to receive regulatory approval in December. Completion and transfer of ownership are anticipated to occur at that time.

As part of the transaction, a lease agreement will be signed with ViaCon UK, which will remain as tenant of the property. The sale is expected to generate a positive cash flow impact of approximately EUR 6 million in the fourth quarter and result in a consolidated capital gain of around EUR 3 million before tax.

data.title: Fitch Ratings: Shortened Tail Period a Concern in EMEA CLOs
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-London-03 December 2025: There has been a noticeable shortening of tail periods in EMEA CLOs, with the period from the weighted average life (WAL) test end-date to the stated maturity date of the notes to less than four years, Fitch Ratings says. This shortening is mainly as a result of reducing the time to legal final maturity from traditionally 13 or more years to 12 years. While we understand that they may be the result of investor requests as longer maturity may affect the cost of funds, Fitch is concerned with the accumulation of long-dated assets and forced sales just before the legal final maturity.

Many legacy EMEA CLO transactions from the 2019 vintage and earlier continue to reinvest despite having passed the reinvestment end date and failing their WAL test. An increasing number of transactions have developed long-dated buckets owing to loans being amended and extended past the legal final maturity of the CLO, with some growing to significant exposure due to deleveraging. While more recent transactions include haircuts to these assets for the calculation of overcollateralisation tests, that is not the case for the majority of these legacy transactions.

The long-dated obligations would have to be sold by the manager regardless of their market value, at the latest by the legal final maturity date. While managers would aim to sell these earlier, especially with the haircut applied in overcollateralisation tests, we have also seen very volatile market values in the loan market. While better credits trade above par, this can change rapidly as the example of First Brands group default illustrates. With the legal final maturity of the CLO approaching the manager may not have time to wait for a recovery in market values.

The shortening of the legal final maturity to reduce the tail period between the WAL end date and the legal final to less than four years increases the risk further that these transactions may accumulate a long- dated bucket through extensions. Fitch would assume an extension in line with other deals with short tail periods of less than four years.

This risk will be particularly acute for the notes rated lower in the capital structure which remain outstanding for longer and whose repayment is more dependent on the sale of long-dated assets. This is also the reason why structures have shorter maturity dates, e.g. 6 months shorter for the ‘AAA’ notes than other rated notes as in the case for Adagio IV CLO DAC: this accommodates investor preference for a shorter maturity and for balancing the risks of a shorter tail.

data.title: Snowflake Stock Has Had A Strong Year. Earnings Could Push It Even Higher. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Snowflake stock has soared as investors bet that the cloud-based storage company will continue to benefit from artificial intelligence investments. Earnings could help reaffirm that positive sentiment.

Snowflake, which is a leading vendor of data warehouse and cloud data management solutions, is scheduled to report third-quarter financial results after the market closes on Wednesday. Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect the company to post adjusted earnings of 31 cents a share on revenue of $1.18 billion.

In the same period last year, Snowflake posted adjusted earnings of 20 cents a share on revenue of $942.1 million.

"We believe the combination of Big Cloud's strong results, encouraging consumption patterns at software vendors with usage-based models, and Snowflake's accelerated pace of innovation, positions the company well in the coming quarters," Monness, Crespi, Hardt analyst Benjamin White wrote in a note on Nov. 25. He rates the stock as a Buy with a $260 price target.

Snowflake stock has jumped 68% this year to about $260 and is on pace for its best calendar year on record, according to Dow Jones Market Data. The company continues to benefit from growing AI demand as businesses need a place to organize, store, and secure their data.

However, shares have dropped 5.8% from their 2025 peak of $277.14 on Nov. 3. Tech stocks have recently faced concerns that there's a market bubble as companies continue to spend big on AI, but aren't yet seeing big tangible returns on those investments.

On top of bubble worries, investors are paying close attention to competition that Snowflake faces, specifically with a company like Databricks. D.A. Davidson analyst Gil Luria, however, is not concerned.

"Snowflake is seeing notable tailwinds in this Software environment, with little threat from Databricks," he wrote in a note on Monday.

"We continue to hear that both companies have plenty of room in their own respective swim lanes despite the two dipping their toes into each other's spaces," Luria added. He raised his price target on Snowflake stock to $300 from $275 and maintained a Buy rating on the stock.

Shares of Snowflake were up 0.1% Wednesday to $260.04.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

U.S. Services Index Unexpectedly Indicates Slightly Faster Growth In November
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Service sector activity in the U.S. unexpectedly grew at a slightly faster rate in the month of November, according to a report released by the Institute for Supply Management on Wednesday.

The ISM said its services PMI inched up to 52.6 in November after climbing to 52.4 in October, with a reading above 50 indicating growth. Economists had expected the index to edge down to 52.1.

With the unexpected increase, the services PMI reached its highest level since hitting 53.5 in February.

The uptick by the headline index came amid a modest increase by the business activity index, which crept up to 54.5 in November from 54.3 in October.

The employment index also rose to 48.9 in November from 48.2 in October. While still in contraction territory, the index reached its highest reading since hitting 50.7 in May.

The report also said the backlog of orders index surged to 49.1 in November from 40.8 in October, marking its largest single-month increase since June 2022.

Meanwhile, the ISM said the new orders slid to 52.9 in November from 56.2 in October, although the reading above 50 still indicated the six straight month of growth.

'The continued expansion in both the Business Activity and New Orders indexes in November, and the highest Backlog of Orders index reading since February 2025 are positive signs of an emerging recovery for the services sector,' said Steve Miller, Chair of the ISM Services Business Survey Committee.

He added, 'On the downside, tariffs and the government shutdown continue to be noted by survey respondents as impacting both demand and costs.'

On the inflation front, the report said the prices index slumped to 65.4 in November from 70.0 in October, hitting its lowest reading since April.

The ISM released a separate report on Monday unexpectedly showing a modest decrease by its reading on U.S. manufacturing activity in the month of November.

The report said manufacturing PMI slipped to 48.2 in November from 48.7 in October, with a reading below 50 indicating contraction. Economists had expected the index to inch up to 49.0.

Copyright(c) 2025 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Glencore Outlines Copper Growth Plan, Confirms Alumbrera Mine Restart
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Glencore Plc (GLEN.L) on Wednesday announced major progress on its copper strategy at its 2025 Capital Markets Day, outlining a pathway to produce about 1 million tons of copper by 2028 and targeting roughly 1.6 million tons by 2035.

The company is looking at a 4 percent annual growth rate for copper-equivalent production from 2026 to 2029, with copper production expected to rise by about 9.4 percent each year.

Glencore also announced plans to restart the Alumbrera mine in Argentina, aiming for its first production in 2028.

GLEN.L is currently trading at GBP 381.23, up GBP 20.88 or 5.79 percent on the London Stock Exchange.

data.title: People think lawyers are jerks. Can 'civility oaths' help?
data.description:
Key points:
  • California is first state to require annual civility pledge from attorneys
  • Only 17% of Americans rate lawyers as highly honest
  • Few states discipline lawyers for uncivil behavior

By Karen Sloan

California in 2026 will become the first U.S. state to require its attorneys to take an annual civility oath to “conduct themselves with dignity, courtesy, and integrity” — the latest attempt to rein in obnoxious lawyers.

Pugnacious and sharp-elbowed attorneys make for compelling movies and television, but off the screen academics and judges say incivility is contributing to high rates of anxiety and depression among lawyers, exacerbating the public’s low opinion of them and eroding trust in the justice system.

Incivility is “sand in the gears” of the legal system, said Brian Currey, a retired California appeals court judge and the chair of the 2021 California task force that proposed the annual civility oath. “When lawyers waste their time and energy sniping at each other, it makes resolving disputes or issues more difficult,” he told Reuters.

Under California's new requirement, approved by the Supreme Court of California in September and finalized by the State Bar of California last month, lawyers must reaffirm their civility pledge every time they pay their annual state bar dues.

Lawyers are losing in the court of public opinion. A 2025 Gallup survey found that just 17% of the American public rated lawyers as having high honesty and ethics — significantly lower than teachers, doctors and police officers, albeit higher than car salespeople and members of Congress. Uncivil lawyer behavior plays into those low approval ratings, professors and judges said.

It’s not hard to find “obstreperous jerks” in courthouses, law firm offices and other legal settings, said David Grenardo, a professor at the University of St. Thomas School of Law who studies lawyer civility. A 2021 survey by the Illinois Supreme Court Commission on Professionalism found that 54% of lawyer respondents experienced “uncivil or unprofessional behavior” from another lawyer during the previous six months. Nearly a quarter of lawyers were victims of workplace bullying in the past year, with 18% saying they had left a law job due to bullying, according to a 2024 study from the same commission.

California’s task force concluded that lawyers from marginalized groups, including women and people of color, are “disproportionately on the receiving end” of uncivil conduct.

Incivility is a grayer area than attorney misconduct, which involves violations of professional and ethical duties and can lead to discipline or disbarment. Examples of lawyer incivility include derogatory comments, personal insults, burying opposing counsel in unnecessary requests, and “playing hardball” like refusing to accommodate opposing counsel’s reasonable scheduling requests, according to judges and law professors.

“Incivility in the courtroom is when it becomes personal,” said David Keenan, a Washington state trial judge in Seattle. “It’s when lawyers start attacking their opponent rather than the law.”

Clients contribute to the problem by seeking out “bulldogs,” cuing lawyers to be overly aggressive even if that approach doesn’t yield the best result, said Paula Lustbader, a law professor who leads Seattle University School of Law’s Civility Center for the Law.

The legal profession has been trying to improve lawyer behavior for decades. Former U.S. Supreme Court Justice Sandra Day O’Connor implored lawyers to be more civil in a series of 1990 speeches, saying greater civility “can only enhance the pleasure lawyers find in practice, increase the effectiveness of our system of justice, and improve the public's perception of lawyers."

California, which has the nation’s second-largest lawyer population behind New York, is one of 24 states that require lawyers to take a one-time civility oath when they are first admitted to practice, according to Grenardo. Since 2023, attorneys in the state must also complete one hour of civility-focused continuing legal education every three years.

But such pledges are carrots without sticks, said Grenardo, who advocates for stronger enforcement mechanisms to rein in uncivil lawyers.

Only five states — Florida, Michigan, South Carolina, Oklahoma and Arizona — explicitly allow lawyers to be disciplined for uncivil conduct, he said, though some judges have lowered attorney fees or disqualified lawyers from cases due to incivility.

California’s task force recommended adding incivility as grounds for attorney discipline, but the California Supreme Court rejected that proposal, citing free speech and vagueness concerns.

“I have written and talked about this for about 15 years and I have seen so many calls for civility,” Grenardo said. “The only way to root out systemic incivility in the legal profession is to make it mandatory.”
